claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

Do you have a child or children you're responsible for? If yes, could you tell me:
1. How many children you're asking about?
2. Their names and ages?

This will help me determine which eligibility path applies to your situation. Children under 16 generally qualify automatically, while those aged 16-19 need to meet certain education or training requirements, and those 20 or over typically don't qualify.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm asking about my son Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me work through Alex's eligibility.

Since Alex is 8 years old, he falls into the **under 16** category. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's work situation:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since he's 8 years old, I'm assuming he's not in paid employment, but I need to confirm this for the assessment. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct, he doesn't work at all. He's only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

Again, given his age, I expect the answer is yes (he's not receiving such benefits), but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next check:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This question relates to whether he's enrolled in any apprenticeship program (these rules typically apply to older teens, but I need to check). Given he's 8 years old, I assume he's not in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense for an 8-year-old. Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself financially (and NOT claiming certain benefits like Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these applies to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I normally live in the UK - I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You meet this requirement if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** - paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards his care, including things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit payment

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me, but I do contribute to his upkeep - I help with clothes and school dinners.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. To confirm that your contributions meet the requirement, I need to check if they equal or exceed the Child Benefit amount.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see that the specific Child Benefit payment amounts aren't listed in the constants, but according to current UK government rates:
- The eldest/only child receives **£25.60 per week** (approximately £110 per month)
- Additional children receive **£16.95 per week each**

For you to be considered responsible for Alex through contributions, you need to contribute **at least the equivalent of £25.60 per week** (about £110 per month) towards his upkeep. This can include:
- Clothes
- Food (including school dinners)
- Pocket money
- Housing costs
- Presents
- Other care costs

**Do your contributions for Alex (including clothes and school dinners) add up to at least £25.60 per week or about £110 per month?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £30 a week towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Your contribution of £30 per week exceeds the Child Benefit amount, so you meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time. 

- If no one else is claiming, we can proceed
- If someone else is claiming but they agree to transfer it to you, that's fine
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the final decision

What's the situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?**

1. **In hospital** - Is Alex currently in hospital?
2. **In care** - Is Alex in local authority care?
3. **Fostered** - Is Alex in a foster care arrangement?
4. **Normal living** - Is Alex living in a normal family situation (not in hospital, care, or fostered)?

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's in a normal living situation - he lives with his mum.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for where Alex lives or contributing to his upkeep? Generally, if the local council is paying for the child, you cannot get Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me finalize the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex.

### Summary of Your Eligibility:

✅ **Alex is a qualifying child** (8 years old, under 16)  
✅ **You meet UK residency requirements** (normally live in Manchester)  
✅ **You are responsible for Alex** (contributing £30/week towards his upkeep)  
✅ **No other claimant** for Alex  
✅ **No disqualifying factors** present

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of it back through your tax return (this doesn't affect your eligibility, just whether you ultimately keep the full payment)
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Current Child Benefit Rates:
- **Eldest/only child**: £25.60 per week (approximately £110 per month)
- **Additional children**: £16.95 per week each

---

**To apply, visit:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: 1) He is 8 years old (under 16, qualifying age), 2) He is not in paid work, 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right, 4) He is not in an apprenticeship, 5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements, 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £30 per week towards his upkeep (exceeding the £25.60 weekly Child Benefit amount), 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, 8) Alex lives in a normal family situation with his mother, and 9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. All qualifying criteria have been met including the child's age, the claimant's residency status, financial responsibility for the child, and no disqualifying factors are present.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: 1) He is 8 years old (under 16, qualifying age), 2) He is not in paid work, 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right, 4) He is not in an apprenticeship, 5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements, 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £30 per week towards his upkeep (exceeding the £25.60 weekly Child Benefit amount), 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, 8) Alex lives in a normal family situation with his mother, and 9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. All qualifying criteria have been met including the child's age, the claimant's residency status, financial responsibility for the child, and no disqualifying factors are present.
